title: Lap 4 — Expert Challenge
description: The ultimate test. Advanced DQL, Davis AI, automation, and full-stack mastery.
imagine: You are the lead observability architect at a global fintech running thousands of microservices. A silent degradation is spreading across regions and your SLO burn rate is accelerating. The team is looking to you.
yourGoal: Answer 5 expert-level questions that span DQL, Davis AI causal analysis, SLO error budgets, distributed tracing, and Dynatrace automation to resolve the incident before the burn rate breaches the 30-day budget.
tools: Dynatrace Platform · DQL (Grail) · Davis AI · SLO Dashboards · Dynatrace Workflows
note: This lap assumes hands-on experience with Dynatrace. Questions require reasoning across multiple observability dimensions simultaneously — no single signal tells the whole story.

story.introduction: At expert level, the problem is never just one thing. A spike in error rate is a symptom. Davis AI surfaces the cause. DQL lets you confirm the blast radius. Automation remediates before the page even fires.
story.context: You have full access to the Dynatrace platform. Every answer requires you to think like an SRE who owns the entire stack — from infrastructure anomalies to business impact.

questions.title: DQL Aggregation
questions.content: You need to find the top 3 services by average response time in the last 15 minutes using DQL. Which clause correctly limits and sorts the result?

options.text: | sort avg(duration) desc | limit 3
options.explanation: Correct! In DQL you pipe sort before limit. sort avg(duration) desc orders highest first, then limit 3 takes the top three.

options.text: | limit 3 | sort avg(duration) desc
options.explanation: Limiting before sorting returns an arbitrary 3 rows and then sorts only those — not the global top 3.

options.text: | top 3 by avg(duration)
options.explanation: DQL does not have a 'top N by' syntax. Use sort + limit.

options.text: | order by avg(duration) desc fetch first 3 rows only
options.explanation: That is SQL syntax. DQL uses sort and limit, not ORDER BY / FETCH FIRST.

questions.explanation: DQL pipeline: summarize → sort → limit. Always sort before limiting to guarantee you get the global top N.
hints: Think of DQL as a pipeline — each step transforms the result of the previous one. Order matters.

questions.title: Davis AI Root Cause
questions.content: Davis AI opens a problem card linking a memory leak on a host to an increase in response times across 4 downstream services. What is the correct interpretation?

options.text: The 4 services are the root cause — they are consuming too much memory
options.explanation: Davis identified the host memory leak as the root cause, not the downstream services.

options.text: The memory leak on the host is the root cause; the service degradations are causal impacts
options.explanation: Correct! Davis AI performs causal analysis — it links the upstream root cause (memory leak) to downstream symptoms (response time increases) automatically.

options.text: All 5 entities are equally responsible and must be fixed simultaneously
options.explanation: Davis establishes causality, not equal blame. Fix the root cause and the downstream symptoms typically resolve.

options.text: The problem card is cosmetic — you must manually correlate events to find the real cause
options.explanation: Davis AI performs automated causal analysis. The problem card reflects real causal relationships, not just correlation.

questions.explanation: Davis AI distinguishes root causes from causal impacts. The entity with the anomaly that triggered all others is the root cause — here, the memory leak on the host.
hints: Look at which entity Davis flagged first in the timeline and which others it linked as 'impacted by'.

questions.title: SLO Error Budget
questions.content: Your payment service has a 99.9% availability SLO over a 30-day window. It has been down for 35 minutes this month. Approximately how much error budget remains?

options.text: About 8 minutes remaining
options.explanation: 99.9% over 30 days = 43.2 minutes budget. 43.2 − 35 = 8.2 minutes. Close — but check the calculation below.

options.text: About 43 minutes remaining
options.explanation: 43 minutes is the full budget. You have already consumed 35 of those minutes.

options.text: About 8 minutes remaining
options.explanation: Correct! 30 days = 43,200 minutes. 0.1% of that = 43.2 minutes budget. 43.2 − 35 = 8.2 minutes left.

options.text: The budget is already exhausted
options.explanation: 35 minutes consumed out of a 43.2-minute budget — you still have ~8 minutes before the SLO is breached.

questions.explanation: 99.9% SLO over 30 days allows 0.1% downtime = 43.2 minutes. With 35 minutes consumed, ~8 minutes of error budget remain.
hints: Error budget = (1 − SLO target) × window duration. Convert everything to the same unit first.

questions.title: Distributed Tracing
questions.content: A trace shows a 2-second span on the checkout service with a 1.8-second child span on the inventory service. Where is the bottleneck?

options.text: Checkout service — it owns the longest total span
options.explanation: The checkout span includes the time waiting for inventory. Its own processing time is only 0.2 seconds.

options.text: Inventory service — it consumes 1.8 of the 2 seconds
options.explanation: Correct! The inventory child span accounts for 90% of the total request time. That is where the bottleneck lives.

options.text: The network between the two services
options.explanation: Network latency would appear as a gap between spans. The 1.8s is within the inventory span itself.

options.text: Cannot tell without CPU metrics
options.explanation: The trace gives you enough information. Span duration directly shows where time is being spent.

questions.explanation: In distributed tracing, the child span with the largest duration relative to the parent is the bottleneck. 1.8s out of 2s = 90% of time in inventory.
hints: Calculate what percentage of the parent span each child span consumes — the highest percentage is your bottleneck.

questions.title: Dynatrace Workflows
questions.content: You want to automatically restart a Kubernetes pod when Davis AI opens a problem for that specific service. Which Dynatrace capability enables this?

options.text: Dynatrace Dashboards with threshold alerts
options.explanation: Dashboards visualise data but cannot trigger automated remediation actions.

options.text: Dynatrace Workflows triggered by a Davis AI problem event
options.explanation: Correct! Dynatrace Workflows can be triggered by Davis AI problem events and can execute actions — including calling the Kubernetes API to restart a pod.

options.text: A custom metric alert sent to PagerDuty
options.explanation: PagerDuty notifies a human — it does not automatically restart the pod. You still need a human in the loop.

options.text: Dynatrace SLO burn rate alert
options.explanation: SLO alerts notify you when a budget is burning — they do not execute remediation actions automatically.

questions.explanation: Dynatrace Workflows connect Davis AI events to automated actions. A problem-triggered workflow can call external APIs, run scripts, or interact with orchestration platforms like Kubernetes.
hints: Which Dynatrace feature is specifically designed to automate actions in response to platform events?
